playstyles.name: Neutral
playstyles.summary: For players who enjoy controlling space, winning repeated interactions, and making the opponent take awkward approaches.
recommendedShells: Neutral control plus a reliable cashout character.
Beam or screen-control support behind a point that wants safer approaches.
A self-sufficient mid or anchor that can stabilize the match after the first character falls.
strengths: Often creates stable game plans.
Can slow down volatile matchups.
Makes team order and resource use easier to understand.
weaknesses: May need a dedicated mix or damage character to close rounds.
Can become too passive if the team lacks a clear conversion plan.
playstyles.whyItWorks: The style works by making approaches predictable, then using assists, DHCs, or meter to turn clean neutral wins into repeatable advantage.

playstyles.name: Footsies
playstyles.summary: For players who like grounded spacing, whiff punishment, clean buttons, and patient interaction wins.
recommendedShells: A grounded point with assists that cover air approaches.
Strong neutral characters backed by a high-reward anchor.
A fundamentals core that does not need extreme setplay to function.
strengths: Rewards clean decision making.
Can stay useful across many matchups.
Usually avoids overly fragile team plans.
weaknesses: May struggle if the opponent ignores spacing with strong mobility.
Needs enough reward to make small wins matter.
playstyles.whyItWorks: The style works by forcing the opponent to overextend, then converting controlled interactions into corner carry, oki, or resource advantage.

playstyles.name: Rushdown
playstyles.summary: For players who want to move first, create pressure quickly, and keep turns active.
recommendedShells: Fast point plus lockdown assist.
Pressure character plus high-damage cashout.
Momentum point plus anchor that can spend the meter created by long offense.
strengths: Can overwhelm defensive hesitation.
Often builds meter through extended pressure.
Works well with simple team goals.
weaknesses: Can overextend without neutral cover.
Needs assists or routing to keep pressure meaningful.
playstyles.whyItWorks: The style works by forcing frequent defensive decisions and making each blocked sequence or stray hit lead into another advantage state.

playstyles.name: Mix
playstyles.summary: For players who enjoy layered offense, left-rights, high-lows, strike-throw, and making defense uncomfortable.
recommendedShells: Mix point plus high blockstun assist.
Character with strong oki plus partners that improve knockdown reward.
Assist-backed offense plus a character that converts mix into high damage.
strengths: Can win from fewer neutral wins.
Rewards team-specific setup knowledge.
Pairs well with lockdown and safe-tag support.
weaknesses: Can become assist-dependent.
Needs practice to make setups consistent.
playstyles.whyItWorks: The style works when the team repeatedly turns blockstun, knockdowns, or level 3 situations into difficult defensive guesses.

playstyles.name: Setplay
playstyles.summary: For players who enjoy prepared situations, setup routes, oki, and character-specific sequences.
recommendedShells: Setup character plus lockdown or screen-control assist.
Corner-carry point into a setplay mid.
Setplay character plus anchor that can cash out when the setup opens the opponent.
strengths: Can create very specific win conditions.
Rewards lab time and route knowledge.
Often gives the team a unique identity.
weaknesses: Can require more matchup and routing knowledge.
May need neutral support before the setup begins.
playstyles.whyItWorks: The style works by using a previous hit, knockdown, or tag route to start a planned situation where the team already knows the next layers.

playstyles.name: Zoning
playstyles.summary: For players who enjoy projectiles, screen control, slowing the match down, and making approaches difficult.
recommendedShells: Zoner plus defensive or tracking assist.
Projectile control plus a high-reward anchor.
Screen-control shell that can still cash out when the opponent finally gets clipped.
strengths: Controls pace and screen position.
Can frustrate linear approaches.
Often pairs naturally with strong anchors.
weaknesses: Needs answers when opponents get in.
Can lack immediate mix if the team is too control-heavy.
playstyles.whyItWorks: The style works by making the opponent navigate projectiles and assists before they can start offense, then punishing rushed movement.

playstyles.name: Defensive
playstyles.summary: For players who prefer patience, reversals, counters, barriers, and stable decision making.
recommendedShells: Defensive point backed by a reliable neutral assist.
Barrier or counter utility next to a character with strong reward.
Stable neutral shell with a comeback anchor.
strengths: Can reduce risky scramble situations.
Rewards strong reads and patience.
Often stays functional even when behind.
weaknesses: May need a clear way to start offense.
Can become too reactive against teams with strong setplay.
playstyles.whyItWorks: The style works by making the opponent commit first, then using defensive utility, spacing, or assists to regain control.

playstyles.name: Beginner Friendly
playstyles.summary: For players who want stable tools, clear roles, and teams that teach fundamentals without requiring too much specialized routing.
recommendedShells: Generalist point plus simple support assists.
Beam or neutral assist plus a clear anchor.
Balanced team with one character for neutral, one for pressure, and one for cashout.
strengths: Clear learning path.
Good for understanding team roles.
Less dependent on niche setups.
weaknesses: May not maximize specialized win conditions.
Can feel less explosive than technical shells.
playstyles.whyItWorks: The style works by giving the player reliable tools first, making it easier to understand neutral, assists, routing, and team order.

playstyles.name: Technical
playstyles.summary: For players who enjoy complex routes, unique resources, setup timing, and unusual character mechanics.
recommendedShells: Technical point plus assists that stabilize setup timing.
Character-specific DHC or tag routes built around one central payoff.
Setup character plus simpler partners that cover neutral and cashout.
strengths: High ceiling for lab work.
Can create uncommon matchup problems.
Often gives the player a very personal team identity.
weaknesses: Can be harder to maintain under pressure.
Requires more route and matchup knowledge.
playstyles.whyItWorks: The style works when unique mechanics are supported by partners that make the difficult parts more consistent.

playstyles.name: Anchor Focused
playstyles.summary: For players who like building toward a strong late-game character with meter, Spark, and comeback value.
recommendedShells: Point and mid generate resources for the final character.
Stable first two characters plus an anchor with solo neutral or huge meter spend.
Control shell that keeps the anchor stocked for late-game conversions.
strengths: Clear resource plan.
Can stay threatening late in the match.
Makes recommended order easier to explain.
weaknesses: Can overvalue comeback plans if the first two characters lack pressure.
Needs enough meter generation or neutral stability to reach the desired state.
playstyles.whyItWorks: The style works by using the first part of the team to build meter, control, or damage routes, then letting the final character convert those resources.
